content.problemStatement:
The goodness of a permutation is the **maximum** integer kkk such that the permutation can be split‡{^\ddag}‡ into **exactly** kkk subarrays where each subarray has the **same sum**.

You are given an integer NNN. Generate a permutation of length NNN which has **maximum** goodness.

If multiple permutations with maximum goodness exist, print any.

†{^\dag}†A permutation of length NNN consists of all integers from 111 to NNN in any order.

‡{^\ddag}‡A split of permutation is the distribution of the elements of permutation such that each element lies inside **exactly one** subarray. For example, for a permutation [4,1,5,3,2][4, 1, 5, 3, 2][4,1,5,3,2]; {[4,1],[5,3],[2]}\{[4, 1], [5, 3], [2]\}{[4,1],[5,3],[2]} is a split whereas {[4,1],[3],[2]}\{[4, 1], [3], [2]\}{[4,1],[3],[2]} and {[4,1,2],[5,3]}\{[4, 1, 2], [5, 3]\}{[4,1,2],[5,3]} are not.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a single integer NNN — the length of the required permutation.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, NNN space-separated integers, denoting a permutation of length NNN with maximum goodness.


If multiple permutations with maximum goodness exist, print any.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 10^6
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 10^5).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line with N space‑separated integers – a permutation of {1,…,N} whose goodness is maximum.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For N=1 the only permutation is [1] (goodness 1). For N=2 the permutation [1,2] gives one subarray of sum 3, which is maximal. For N=5 the construction yields [1,5,2,4,3]; it can be split into {[1,5],[2,4],[3]} each sum 6, achieving goodness floor((5+1)/2)=3.
N=4 → pairs (1,4) and (2,3) each sum 5, goodness 2. N=6 → pairs (1,6),(2,5),(3,4) each sum 7, goodness 3.
Pairs sum to 10; five subarrays {[1,9],[2,8],[3,7],[4,6],[5]} each sum 10, goodness floor((9+1)/2)=5.

solution.approach: The total sum of numbers 1..N is S = N(N+1)/2. The goodness k must divide S and each subarray must have the same sum S/k. The largest possible k is floor((N+1)/2). This value is always attainable: pair the smallest unused number i with the largest unused number N+1‑i; each pair sums to N+1. For odd N one element ((N+1)/2) remains and itself equals N+1. By arranging the permutation as [1, N, 2, N‑1, 3, N‑2, …] (and the middle element at the end when N is odd) we obtain exactly floor((N+1)/2) contiguous blocks, each of sum N+1, which is the maximum possible goodness. The construction is linear in N.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, overall O(total N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra besides the output array.
